label: steelman-poles
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: identify the poles only. State (1) the conclusion most California
public-agency lawyers would reach here, and (2) the opposing position that
deserves construction. State the disfavored position neutrally and WITHOUT
supporting reasoning — someone else will build the case and must not be
anchored by yours.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

Identify the conventional view and the disfavored position.

conventional_view.description: the conclusion most California public-agency lawyers would reach, in 1-3 sentences

disfavored_position.description: the opposing position that deserves serious construction, stated neutrally in 1-3 sentences, WITHOUT any supporting reasoning

text: {"conventional_view":"Most California public-agency lawyers would conclude Okafor has a disqualifying financial interest \"in\" the contract under § 1090 — both through the spouse's community-property salary interest (see Family Code § 760; People v. Honig; Thomson v. Call) and independently through uncompensated board membership on a bidding nonprofit — and that no § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception cleanly reaches a spouse's compensated employment with the bidder. Because § 1090's prohibition and voidness remedy attach to the whole governmental body/contract rather than being cured by one member's abstention (Stigall v. City of Taft; Thomson v. Call), recusal alone does not save an award to Harbor Light; the conventional advice is that Okafor must resign the board seat and the spousal financial nexus to Harbor Light must be eliminated (or Harbor Light must be excluded from this particular procurement) before the city can safely contract with it, in addition to mandatory PRA § 87100/87103 recusal from both the framework and award votes.","disfavored_position":"The city may lawfully contract with Harbor Light if Okafor simply recuses from both the RFP-framework vote and the award vote (with full disclosure), without resigning the board seat and without any change to the spouse's employment — on the view that the board seat and/or spousal salary interest qualifies for treatment as a remote interest under § 1091 or a noninterest under § 1091.5, that the salary is fixed and not contingent on this specific contract, and that recusal satisfies both § 1090 and the Political Reform Act's materiality and public-generally provisions in this setting."}

data.conventional_view: Most California public-agency lawyers would conclude Okafor has a disqualifying financial interest "in" the contract under § 1090 — both through the spouse's community-property salary interest (see Family Code § 760; People v. Honig; Thomson v. Call) and independently through uncompensated board membership on a bidding nonprofit — and that no § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception cleanly reaches a spouse's compensated employment with the bidder. Because § 1090's prohibition and voidness remedy attach to the whole governmental body/contract rather than being cured by one member's abstention (Stigall v. City of Taft; Thomson v. Call), recusal alone does not save an award to Harbor Light; the conventional advice is that Okafor must resign the board seat and the spousal financial nexus to Harbor Light must be eliminated (or Harbor Light must be excluded from this particular procurement) before the city can safely contract with it, in addition to mandatory PRA § 87100/87103 recusal from both the framework and award votes.
data.disfavored_position: The city may lawfully contract with Harbor Light if Okafor simply recuses from both the RFP-framework vote and the award vote (with full disclosure), without resigning the board seat and without any change to the spouse's employment — on the view that the board seat and/or spousal salary interest qualifies for treatment as a remote interest under § 1091 or a noninterest under § 1091.5, that the salary is fixed and not contingent on this specific contract, and that recusal satisfies both § 1090 and the Political Reform Act's materiality and public-generally provisions in this setting.
